applies_when: User asks for a room near the metro, old town, mall district, arena, or a citywalk area without giving dates, guest count, or the exact branch
User sounds interested in location convenience first but the stay scope is still broad
items.response_goal: Turn location-led browsing into the minimum stay details needed for a useful city-homestay reply.
do: Acknowledge that the user wants something easy to reach rather than a long sales script
Ask only for the next critical details such as area or landmark, stay dates, guest count, and whether they care more about metro access, walkability, or room layout
Keep the tone practical and human instead of listing every room type too early
avoid: Claiming broad location convenience without knowing the exact property
Dumping room options before the stay basics are known
Sounding like a generic booking bot
items.safe_transition: If you send the area or landmark you want plus the dates and how many people are staying, I can help narrow the next step much more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Location-fit clarification lead if the user shares district and stay details.

applies_when: User asks for a room tonight or asks whether self check-in is still possible after a late arrival
User wants reassurance about access code, building entry, or host response during the same day
items.response_goal: Calm arrival pressure first and move the conversation toward workable same-day check-in details instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that late arrival uncertainty can feel stressful
Ask for the exact property, arrival window, whether the booking already exists, and how many guests are coming
Frame the answer around live room status, self check-in flow, and property-specific access handling instead of certainty
avoid: Guaranteeing that a code will definitely be sent instantly
Acting as if every city homestay supports fully unattended check-in at any hour
Using pressure language such as book now or lose it
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you mean and roughly what time you expect to arrive, I can point you to the safer next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Same-day arrival with timing details should move quickly toward reservation or host confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether the homestay is suitable after a concert, game, nightlife outing, or a very late return
User wants to know whether several guests can come back late without access trouble
items.response_goal: Keep late-return questions useful by clarifying venue, timing, and building-access realities before implying a smooth fit.
do: Acknowledge the desire to avoid a messy trip back after the event
Ask for the venue or area, latest expected return time, guest count, and whether the concern is distance, self check-in, or building quiet-hour handling
Use calm boundary language around access, neighborhood noise sensitivity, and live arrival logistics
avoid: Guaranteeing frictionless late return without property context
Encouraging rule-breaking around noise or extra unregistered guests
Treating every building as equally event-friendly
items.safe_transition: If you send the venue, how many people are staying, and the rough return time, I can help narrow the next check more realistically.
items.handoff_suggestion: Late-return stays with event context should route toward access and occupancy confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a loft, two-bedroom, or projector room can fit several adults, children, or a mixed family group
User asks whether extra bedding is possible without sharing headcount, child age, or bed setup needs
items.response_goal: Turn multi-person interest into a realistic occupancy and room-fit conversation without sounding rigid.
do: Acknowledge that group comfort matters more than a simple yes or no
Ask for the number of adults and children, child ages if relevant, and whether the main concern is bed count, privacy, or keeping everyone in one unit
Keep the next question easy to answer in one message
avoid: Promising over-occupancy or extra bedding before the room rules are known
Ignoring stairs, loft layout, or luggage concerns for family guests
Treating a multi-person stay like a one-bed hotel question
items.safe_transition: If you send how many adults and children are coming and what bed setup you need most, I can help narrow which room direction makes sense.
items.handoff_suggestion: Multi-person stays with concrete occupancy details should route as room-fit clarification leads.

applies_when: User asks whether the stay has a kitchen, washer, dryer, projector, bathtub, balcony, or living room setup
User references a feature from a video or listing page but the exact room type is still unclear
items.response_goal: Protect trust by grounding feature questions in the actual room type and stay purpose instead of broad promises.
do: Acknowledge why that feature matters to the stay decision
Ask which room type or listing the user is looking at, how many nights they plan to stay, and which one feature matters most if there are several
Use clear language that amenities can vary by room type, layout, and booking plan
avoid: Assuming every room in the property includes the same home-style amenities
Answering with marketing language only
Treating listing highlights as universal standard equipment
items.safe_transition: If you send the room type or listing you are looking at and the feature you care about most, I can guide the next step more accurately.
items.handoff_suggestion: Amenity-specific questions should route toward room-type clarification.

applies_when: User asks whether the loft, projector wall, balcony, window view, or room size is exactly like the photos or short videos
User sounds skeptical about whether the real room matches social content
items.response_goal: Keep the reply credible by acknowledging expectation risk and moving toward one concrete room detail instead of defending the listing.
do: Acknowledge that real-room accuracy matters before booking
Explain that angle, lighting, floor plan, view direction, and projector effect can vary by room type and live assignment
Invite the user to name the exact detail they want clarified first
avoid: Guaranteeing an exact match to hero photos or videos
Arguing with a skeptical user
Using vague yes wording that creates expectation risk
items.safe_transition: If you tell me whether the key point is the loft space, the window view, the projector setup, or the overall room size, I can help narrow the next check more realistically.

applies_when: User asks whether parking is convenient, whether there is an elevator, or whether the property is hard to reach with luggage
User mentions older guests, a stroller, large suitcases, or a late-night arrival without naming the property
items.response_goal: Protect arrival expectations by clarifying the access details that actually shape the stay experience.
do: Acknowledge the wish to avoid arrival friction
Ask for the property, whether the main concern is parking, stairs, elevator access, or luggage handling, and whether they are driving or arriving by metro or taxi
Mention that access conditions can vary by building, floor, and check-in hour
avoid: Guaranteeing doorstep parking or elevator access without property context
Ignoring vehicle type, luggage, or mobility needs
Treating every city homestay as equally convenient to enter
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you mean and whether the main concern is parking, stairs, or luggage access, I can guide the next step more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Access-sensitive stays should route toward property-specific arrival confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether pets are allowed in the homestay
User asks about pet fee, size limits, cleaning boundaries, or whether a specific room can take a dog or cat
items.response_goal: Keep pet-stay guidance accurate by clarifying the pet and room details that change the answer.
do: Acknowledge that guests want to avoid pet-related friction on arrival
Ask for the property, pet type and size, stay dates, and whether the user already has a room type in mind
Mention that pet handling can vary by room type, cleaning rules, and building boundaries
avoid: Saying pets are always fine without room context
Ignoring size, count, or cleaning boundaries
Overlooking how pet rules affect neighboring guests and room assignment
items.safe_transition: If you tell me what pet you are bringing, when you want to stay, and which room you are considering, I can guide the next check more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Pet-stay questions with concrete details should route to property policy confirmation.

applies_when: User asks for a few nights near a hospital, office district, exam site, or family errand location
User cares about staying a few nights smoothly rather than taking a pure leisure trip
items.response_goal: Keep weekday functional-stay replies practical by clarifying the purpose and the one or two facilities that matter most.
do: Acknowledge that the user is trying to make a short city stay easier, not more complicated
Ask whether the main need is location convenience, a washer, simple cooking, quiet rest, or keeping several people together
Guide toward dates, guest count, and the exact area rather than broad lifestyle talk
avoid: Turning a practical stay into a romanticized travel pitch
Assuming weekday guests care about the same things as weekend citywalk guests
Ignoring operational details like laundry, check-in timing, or luggage
items.safe_transition: If you send the dates, the area you need to be near, and the one facility that matters most, I can help point you to the next check more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Functional weekday stays should be tagged as fit-and-needs clarification leads.

applies_when: User asks whether early check-in or late checkout can definitely be arranged
User has a train, flight, concert, appointment, or family schedule that creates timing pressure
items.response_goal: Show empathy for the timing pressure while avoiding absolute promises around room turnover and host handling.
do: Acknowledge the schedule pressure directly
Explain that early check-in or late checkout can depend on live room status, cleaning progress, and the property's handling flow
Ask for the property, stay date, expected arrival or departure time, and whether the booking already exists
avoid: Guaranteeing time flexibility
Speaking as if every city homestay has hotel-style front-desk coverage
Answering with a vague yes that creates expectation risk
items.safe_transition: If you send the property, stay date, and the time you are trying to arrive or leave, I can help narrow what should be checked next.
items.handoff_suggestion: Timing-flexibility requests should route toward live reservation or host confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a booking can be canceled or refunded
User mentions a deposit, cleaning fee, OTA booking, mini-program booking, or a listing link and says the plan changed or the terms feel unclear
items.response_goal: Protect trust by grounding refund and fee talk in the actual booking terms instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that fee or schedule surprises are frustrating
Ask whether the booking already exists, through which channel it was made, and what the listing or order says about deposit, cleaning fee, or cancellation terms
Guide the user toward the correct policy-check path
avoid: Promising refunds before checking the booking context
Treating direct, OTA, and message-link bookings as identical
Sounding dismissive about confusion over extra fees
items.safe_transition: If you send where the booking was made and what the order or listing says about the fee or cancellation terms, I can help point you to the right next step instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Refund and fee-dispute cases should be routed for booking-policy confirmation.

applies_when: User reports a lock-code problem, cleanliness issue, smell, noisy building, hidden-fee dispute, or room mismatch against the listing
User is visibly upset in public comments or DM about a current or recent city-homestay stay
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first and gather the minimum facts needed for real service recovery.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly and calmly
Move the conversation toward the property, stay date, room or booking details, and the exact issue through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should review the case
avoid: Arguing responsibility in public
Blaming the host, cleaner, platform, or the guest before facts are checked
Requesting excessive personal information in a public thread
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would be frustrating. Please send the property, stay date, and room or booking details by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to reservation support, guest service, or the property host.

applies_when: User is ready to book, complain, or send screenshots in a public comment thread
The next step needs booking details, fee screenshots, check-in codes, or stay information that should be handled privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt or evasive.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with booking, fee, or complaint details
Make the next step specific and low-effort
avoid: Replying only with DM me
Dropping off-platform contact details in public
Using hard-pressure lead capture language
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the property plus your stay or booking details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user is ready to continue privately, notify Catcher and the relevant service path.
